content.assessment: This proposal targets segregation channels (choice/commuting, maintainer exemptions, geographic concentration) that operate independently of the tracking mechanism itself, per evidence distinguishing structural segregation drivers from tracking-driven inequality. It could reduce segregation without disturbing the politically volatile track-age question, but by leaving admissions and track age untouched, it does not address the inequality independently attributed to early tracking itself, so gains would be partial and channel-specific rather than comprehensive.
opportunities: Could be combined with admissions or track-age reforms (as in T3 or T6) for a more comprehensive equity effect
strengths: Targets a documented distinct causal channel: higher inter-school choice commuting correlates with stronger inter-school inequality
Identifies a specific actionable legal lever (anti-discrimination exemption review) with documented precedent of enabling segregated schooling
Avoids the highest-salience political flashpoint of altering track age or admissions design
threats: Prior administrative desegregation rules only produced temporary reductions before stagnating, suggesting limited durability of this lever alone
Legal challenge risk given Curia precedent protecting Church-maintainer segregation exemptions
Centralization under KLIK constrains local planning autonomy needed for zoning-level interventions
weaknesses: Does not address the tracking mechanism itself, which is independently linked to major educational inequality beyond structural/geographic drivers
May leave the core early-selection sorting point untouched even if segregation by geography is reduced
